text: The surgeon applies a wetting solution to the surgical field, then grasps the gallbladder fundus and retracts it posteriorly. Fluid leaks from the gallbladder, which will be suctioned later. A second grasper is introduced under Hartmann's pouch, revealing lymph nodes in the area.

The surgeon seals off the peritoneum from the surrounding tissue, creating a plane to separate the gallbladder from the liver fossa. The gallbladder is visibly being dissected posteriorly, revealing a cleavage plane, while the cystic artery is located anteriorly.

The surgeon performs a conventional dissection of the gallbladder from the liver, identifying and dissecting the large cystic artery and cystic duct. The dissection reveals a lymph node with signs of acute inflammation. The cystic artery is dissected, and the separation of the cystic duct and artery is achieved both posteriorly and anteriorly.

The surgical field shows the liver fossa with the cystic duct and cystic artery clearly visible. The critical view of safety is achieved, with the cystic duct and artery distinctly separated. Clips are applied to the cystic duct and artery to secure them, ensuring no unnecessary fascia removal is needed.

The surgeon places clips on the cystic duct, ensuring complete obliteration by positioning them above the common bile duct (CBD). Multiple clips are applied to secure the duct, considering the junction's thickness due to the patient's previous jaundice.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct (CBD) and observes the pulsation of the cystic artery and cystic duct. The gallbladder is being separated from the liver bed, with visible inflammatory tissue present. The critical view of safety is partially achieved, and the gallbladder is gradually being dissected from the liver fossa.

The surgeon uses a spatula to coagulate the tissue at the posterior wall of the gallbladder fossa. The gallbladder is pushed back temporarily, revealing a thick wall with stones inside. Scissors and a spatula are used to open the gallbladder, allowing stones to fall out, which are then removed.

The surgeon uses a finger to manipulate and inspect the surgical area, ensuring no small stones remain. The camera is cleaned and repositioned. Suction is applied to remove any remaining fluids, and the gallbladder is drawn out. The cystic duct and cystic artery are visible, with no stones left. A drain is placed before concluding the procedure.
A small amount of glue and a fragment of stone are visible in the surgical field. The area is clear with no collection errors, and adjustments have been completed.
